---
title: "Unit 2b — Probability & Estimation Foundations (II)"
subtitle: "Matrix calculus (Jacobian identities) · Gaussian independence · MLE unbiasedness · Beta moments"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

This is the **written, derivation-only** unit that proves the results the rest of the course
relies on. You'll derive the matrix-calculus gradient identities — the ones **Unit 1 already
applied** to get the normal equation — and then a short tour of distributional facts: that a
diagonal-covariance Gaussian factorizes into independent pieces, that the Bernoulli MLE is
unbiased, and the mean and variance of the Beta distribution. Nothing is coded; everything is
shown.

## Learning objectives

By the end of Unit 2b you will be able to:

- **Derive the Jacobian identities** $\partial\mathbf{w}/\partial\mathbf{w}=\mathbf I$,
  $\partial f/\partial\mathbf{w}^\top=(\partial f/\partial\mathbf{w})^\top$, and
  $\partial(\mathbf{Cw})/\partial\mathbf{w}^\top=\mathbf C$. *(Apply/Analyze)*
- **Show a diagonal-covariance Gaussian** factorizes into independent univariate Gaussians.
  *(Analyze)*
- **Prove the Bernoulli MLE is unbiased**, $\mathbb{E}[\hat r]=r$. *(Apply)*
- **Derive the Beta mean and variance.** *(Analyze)*
- **Interpret**: where symmetry matters, what unbiasedness does and does not guarantee, and
  why the Beta is a natural prior. *(Evaluate)*

## What you'll do

Derive seven results across two parts:

- **Part A** — the three matrix-calculus (Jacobian) identities, from the component /
  Kronecker-delta definitions.
- **Part B** — the diagonal-Gaussian independence factorization, the unbiasedness of the
  Bernoulli MLE, and the Beta$(\alpha,\beta)$ mean and variance (using $\Gamma(n+1)=n\,\Gamma(n)$).

Then answer three interpretation questions. **Any valid route earns full credit**; state every
assumption (symmetry of $\mathbf C$; i.i.d. and $\mathbb{E}[x_n]=r$; $\alpha,\beta>0$).

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** Vector/matrix notation and partial derivatives; the Gaussian and Beta
density forms; expectation and variance; the gamma identity $\Gamma(n+1)=n\,\Gamma(n)$.
Helpful: Unit 2a's MLE (Part B builds on it).

**Connections.**

> This is the **proof** half of Unit 1's *use-then-prove* arc: the identities you derive in
> Part A are exactly the ones Unit 1 applied to reach
> $\widehat{\mathbf{w}}=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{t}$, and identity
> A3 reappears as the **Hessian term in Unit 5**. The Beta mean and variance become the
> **conjugate Beta prior of Unit 4**.

## How it's graded

This unit is **written-only**, so the entire **Correctness** (core/gateway) bundle is graded
**step-by-step by a human** — there is no autograder (the shared classroom config skips
`pytest` when there is no `test_hw.py`). Each derivation step passes at *Proficient* or better.
**Interpretation** scores your CERL answers (Claim, Evidence, Reasoning, Limits) and
**Process** covers consistent notation, stated assumptions, and a clean render. Any unmet
bundle gets one **revise-and-resubmit**. Full policy in [`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md).

## What to submit

- Your **derivation write-ups** — Part A (A1–A3) and Part B (B1–B4), rendered in the unit,
  with every assumption stated. *(This is a written unit — no code or autograder.)*
- Your **interpretation answers** (I1–I3).

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **4–6 hours [PROPOSED]** — seven derivations, all on paper; the work is in being
rigorous and stating assumptions, not in volume.
